sentence: The patient, a 30-year-old female, presented with a chronic history of asthma symptoms, specifically coughing and wheezing, which have been ongoing for the past six months.
She reports using her albuterol inhaler (Ventolin) daily, with a frequency of two puffs, as it is the only medication that provides her with relief.
She has had multiple exacerbations, requiring visits to urgent care and emergency department, with treatment including prednisone, which she has received for the past year.
Her symptoms are triggered by viral infections, and she has noted a specific trigger of her asthma at her aunt's house due to her cat.
She denies any history of smoking, vaping, or marijuana use.
She enjoys outdoor activities such as hiking and walking.
Diminished lung sounds throughout with occasional slight expiratory wheeze, bilaterally
No clubbing on any of her fingertips
Pulmonary function test results are consistent with asthma.
Assessment: The patient's symptoms are consistent with moderate intermittent asthma.
Problem list: Asthma; Asthma (moderate intermittent); Asthma (suspected); [NOT MENTIONED]
I prescribed Flovent 110 micrograms, one puff twice per day to be consistent with her inhaled steroid inhaler to prevent daily symptoms and reduce the need for her albuterol inhaler.
She can continue using her Ventolin inhaler, two puffs every 4-6 hours p.r.n. for acute exacerbations.
I will put together an asthma action plan to help manage her symptoms and identify triggers.
I would like to see her again in a month or sooner if she has any worsening symptoms.
